title: Add tutorials for Flow 1 and Flow 2
description:
docs/flows.md currently describes both login flows as numbered steps and will eventually have Mermaid diagrams (atproto-y1l), but there are no end-to-end tutorials showing developers how to actually implement each flow in a client app.

Add a tutorial for each flow in docs/flows.md (or a new docs/tutorials/ directory), covering:

## Flow 1 tutorial — app has its own email form
- Setting up the /api/oauth/login server endpoint (PAR request with login_hint, DPoP key generation)
- Redirecting to /oauth/authorize with login_hint passthrough
- Handling the callback and exchanging the code for tokens
- Reference the maearth-demo app (/data/projects/maearth-demo) as a working example

## Flow 2 tutorial — app has a login button only
- Setting up the /api/oauth/login server endpoint (PAR request without login_hint)
- Redirecting to /oauth/authorize (no login_hint)
- Handling the callback and exchanging the code for tokens
- Note: Flow 2 is not yet tested end-to-end (atproto-daj) — tutorial should be written once atproto-daj is resolved

## Both tutorials should cover
- Client metadata JSON (what fields are required, where to host it)
- DPoP: key generation, nonce handling, binding tokens
- Error handling (expired request_uri, bad OTP, etc.)
- User handle format (random, not email-derived)
- Code snippets in TypeScript (Next.js API route style, matching maearth-demo)
close_reason: Added tutorials for Flow 1 and Flow 2 to docs/flows.md, with PKCE/DPoP helpers, PAR request, authorization redirect, token exchange, and handle resolution code fragments from maearth-demo. Also closed atproto-daj (Flow 2 works).

description:

The design doc should cover:
- How passkeys fit into the existing OTP-based login flow (alternative to email OTP, not replacement)
- Registration flow: when/where users register passkeys (after first OTP login? dedicated settings page?)
- Authentication flow: how the auth service detects a user has passkeys and offers them
- Integration with better-auth (does better-auth have a passkey plugin, or do we use @simplewebauthn directly?)
- Storage: where passkey credentials are stored (SQLite via EpdsDb? better-auth's DB?)

Reference: maearth-demo has a partial 2FA passkey implementation (src/lib/twofa.ts, src/app/api/twofa/passkey-*) using @simplewebauthn/server + @simplewebauthn/browser, but that's for 2FA verification after login, not as a primary login method. The approach here may differ.

title: Auth service should resolve AT Protocol handles in login_hint to emails
description:
When a third-party ATProto app (e.g. sdsls.dev) initiates an OAuth flow, it sends the user's handle (e.g. user.epds1.test.certified.app) as the login_hint parameter. The auth service only recognizes email-shaped login hints (checks for '@' in login-page.ts:121), so handle-based hints are ignored and the user is shown the email form instead of going straight to OTP.

Fix requires:
1. Accept a handle as a login_hint in the auth service
2. Resolve handle → DID (via DNS/HTTP well-known or XRPC)
3. Look up the DID in the local account database to find the associated email
4. Proceed with the OTP flow using that email

Affected files:
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (login_hint processing at line 121)
- packages/shared/src/db.ts (may need a DID-to-email query)
- Possibly packages/auth-service/src/routes/ (handle resolution utility)
close_reason: PAR login-hint flow working: auth service retrieves login_hint from stored PAR request, resolves handles/DIDs to emails via /_internal/account-by-handle, and skips to OTP. Also fixed missing PDS_INTERNAL_URL causing timeouts in Docker.

title: Wire email sending into better-auth sendVerificationOTP callback
description:
## Context

Part of Phase 2 of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md`.

## Problem

better-auth's emailOTP plugin needs a `sendVerificationOTP` callback to actually deliver OTP codes. The existing `EmailSender` class in `packages/auth-service/src/email/sender.ts` supports SMTP, SendGrid, AWS SES, and Postmark, plus custom client-branded templates. This infrastructure should be reused.

## Solution

Wire the existing `EmailSender.sendOtpCode()` into the `sendVerificationOTP` callback in `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts`.

### Client branding challenge

The current template system needs a `client_id` to resolve branding (logo, colors, email template). During a better-auth emailOTP flow, the `sendVerificationOTP` callback does not have access to the OAuth client context.

Solution: read the `magic_auth_flow` cookie from the request context to recover the `flow_id`, look up the `auth_flow` row to get the `client_id`, and pass it to `emailSender.sendOtpCode()`.

If the request does not have a `magic_auth_flow` cookie (e.g., account settings login), use the default PDS email template.

## Files to modify

- `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts` — wire sendVerificationOTP callback

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] OTP emails delivered via existing EmailSender infrastructure
- [ ] Client branding applied when client_id available from auth_flow
- [ ] Default template used when no client_id context
- [ ] All four email providers (SMTP, SendGrid, SES, Postmark) still work
- [ ] Dev mode (no provider configured) still logs to console
close_reason: Wired existing EmailSender into sendVerificationOTP callback with client branding support. createBetterAuth() now accepts MagicPdsDb; callback reads magic_auth_flow cookie via ctx.getCookie(), looks up auth_flow to get client_id, passes to emailSender.sendOtpCode(). Falls back to default template when no flow context. 6 tests added. 81 tests pass.

title: Passwordless accounts missing account row causes device binding FK failure
description:
When creating a new account via magic-callback, the PDS oauth-store's createAccount() skips registerAccount() when password is undefined (condition: email && passwordScrypt). This leaves the account table empty while actor has a row. The account_device table has a FK on account.did (not actor.did), so upsertDeviceAccount() fails with SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_FOREIGNKEY.

Root cause: packages/pds-core/src/index.ts passes password: undefined to provider.accountManager.createAccount(). The PDS oauth-store.js line ~168 gates registerAccount() on both email AND passwordScrypt being truthy.

Fix applied: after createAccount() returns, manually insert a minimal account row (did, email, passwordScrypt: null) via pds.ctx.accountManager.db before calling upsertDeviceAccount(). This satisfies the FK while keeping the account passwordless.

Also discovered: account_device FK references account.did (not actor.did or device.id separately verified). WAL timing was a red herring — same DB connection is used, the row was simply never written.
notes: Additional finding: account.passwordScrypt has a NOT NULL constraint in the schema, so inserting null fails. Used sentinel value 'passwordless' instead — not a valid scrypt hash so password auth will always fail for these accounts.
close_reason: Fixed by passing a random 256-bit password to createAccount() so registerAccount() runs and the account row is created properly.

title: Eliminate account_email mirror table; use accountManager directly in pds-core
description:
## Context

Part of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` (design decisions 5–6).

## Problem

pds-core currently opens the auth-service's `magic-pds.sqlite` to look up email→DID via an `account_email` mirror table, with a three-step fallback chain:
1. `magicDb.getDidByEmail(email)` — reads `account_email` in magic-pds.sqlite
2. `magicDb.getDidByBackupEmail(email)` — reads `backup_email` in magic-pds.sqlite
3. `magicDb.getDidFromPdsAccount(email)` — opens `account.sqlite` read-only as a fallback

This is redundant because the upstream PDS already stores email→DID in `account.sqlite`, and `pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail()` is a public method that queries it directly.

## Solution

1. In pds-core's `/oauth/magic-callback` handler, replace the `magicDb.getDidByEmail()` + `magicDb.getDidFromPdsAccount()` fallback chain with a single call to `pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail(email)`.

2. Keep the backup email lookup — but have it call `/_internal/account-by-email` or the auth-service's own `backup_email` table via HTTP (backup emails are auth-service-owned data with no PDS equivalent).

3. Remove pds-core's `MagicPdsDb` instantiation entirely — pds-core no longer opens the auth-service database.

4. Delete `getDidByEmail`, `getDidFromPdsAccount`, `setAccountEmail` from `packages/shared/src/db.ts`.

5. Drop the `account_email` table from the auth-service SQLite file.

## Files to modify

- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts` — replace magicDb calls with accountManager; remove MagicPdsDb import and instantiation
- `packages/shared/src/db.ts` — remove getDidByEmail, getDidFromPdsAccount, setAccountEmail, and account_email table creation/migration

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] pds-core no longer imports or instantiates MagicPdsDb
- [ ] pds-core no longer opens magic-pds.sqlite
- [ ] Magic callback uses pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail() for email→DID lookup
- [ ] account_email table dropped from auth-service SQLite schema
- [ ] getDidByEmail, getDidFromPdsAccount, setAccountEmail removed from shared/db.ts
- [ ] New account creation still writes to account.sqlite via createAccount (unchanged)
- [ ] Login flow works end-to-end
close_reason: Removed account_email mirror table from shared/db.ts (v5 migration drops it), removed getDidByEmail/getDidFromPdsAccount/setAccountEmail/getEmailByDid methods. Updated pds-core to use pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail() directly, removed MagicPdsDb instantiation. Updated auth-service verify-code and account-login to query /_internal/account-by-email. All 63 tests pass.

title: Install better-auth and mount alongside existing code
description:
## Context

This is Phase 1 of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` for the full plan.

## Goal

Install better-auth as a dependency, configure it with emailOTP plugin, and mount it on the auth-service Express app alongside the existing routes. No existing behavior changes — this is a foundation-only step.

## Implementation

### 1.1 Install dependencies

```bash
pnpm add better-auth better-sqlite3
# better-sqlite3 is already a dependency — verify version compatibility
```

### 1.2 Create better-auth configuration

Create `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts` with:
- `betterAuth()` instance configured with:
  - `database`: points to `process.env.DB_LOCATION ?? "./data/magic-pds.sqlite"` (shared auth-service SQLite)
  - `baseURL`: `https://${process.env.AUTH_HOSTNAME}`
  - `basePath`: `/api/auth` (namespaced to avoid route conflicts)
  - `emailOTP` plugin with `otpLength: 8`, `expiresIn: 600`, `allowedAttempts: 5`, `storeOTP: "hashed"`, and `sendVerificationOTP` callback wired to existing `EmailSender`
  - `socialProviders` built dynamically from env vars — `buildSocialProviders()` checks for `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`+`GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`, `GITHUB_CLIENT_ID`+`GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET`, etc. Only providers with both vars set are included.
  - `session.expiresIn` from `SESSION_EXPIRES_IN` env var (default 7 days)
  - `session.updateAge` from `SESSION_UPDATE_AGE` env var (default 1 day)
- Export `socialProviders` object so login page can conditionally render buttons

### 1.3 Run better-auth migrations

```bash
pnpm dlx @better-auth/cli migrate
```

Creates `user`, `session`, `account`, `verification` tables in the auth-service SQLite file alongside existing custom tables.

### 1.4 Mount on Express

In `packages/auth-service/src/index.ts`, mount before `express.json()`:

```typescript
import { toNodeHandler } from "better-auth/node";
import { auth } from "./better-auth";
app.all("/api/auth/*", toNodeHandler(auth));
```

Existing routes continue to work unchanged.

## Files to create

- `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts`

## Files to modify

- `packages/auth-service/src/index.ts` — mount better-auth handler
- `packages/auth-service/package.json` — add better-auth dependency
- `.env.example` — add SESSION_EXPIRES_IN, SESSION_UPDATE_AGE, GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] better-auth tables created in auth-service SQLite without breaking existing tables
- [ ] `/api/auth/*` endpoints respond (better-auth health check)
- [ ] Existing OTP login flow still works unchanged
- [ ] No behavior change for users
close_reason: Installed better-auth 1.4.18 and better-sqlite3 as auth-service dependencies. Created packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts with betterAuth() configured with emailOTP plugin (otpLength:8, expiresIn:600, allowedAttempts:5, storeOTP:hashed) wired to existing EmailSender, dynamic social providers from env vars, and session lifetime from env. Mounted at /api/auth/* in index.ts before express.json(). Also converted auth-service to type:module (ESM) to support better-auth which is ESM-only. Fixed SMTPTransport type import in email/sender.ts. Added MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET, SESSION_EXPIRES_IN, SESSION_UPDATE_AGE, GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET, GITHUB_CLIENT_ID/SECRET to .env.example. All 65 tests pass.

title: Migrate account settings portal to better-auth sessions
description:
## Context

Phase 3 of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 3.

## Problem

The `/account/*` routes on the auth-service currently use a custom `account_session` table and `magic_account_session` cookie for session management. This should use better-auth's built-in session system instead.

## Solution

1. Replace `requireAuth(ctx)` calls with a wrapper that calls `auth.api.getSession({ headers })` to validate the better-auth session.

2. The "Account Settings Login" flow becomes: render login page → user authenticates via better-auth → session established → redirect to auth-service `/account`.

3. Session revocation routes delegate to better-auth:
   - `POST /account/session/revoke` → `auth.api.revokeSession()`
   - `POST /account/sessions/revoke-all` → `auth.api.revokeSessions()`

4. These routes stay custom (they call PDS admin APIs):

title: Build auth_flow table and AT Protocol bridge route
description:
## Context

Phase 2 core of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 2.

## Problem

The AT Protocol OAuth flow starts with a PAR `request_uri` that must survive the authentication process and arrive at pds-core's `/oauth/magic-callback`. Currently this is threaded through HTML hidden form fields and stored in the `magic_link_token` DB row. With better-auth, the OTP/social-login flow is opaque — we cannot embed `request_uri` in better-auth's internal state.

## Solution

### auth_flow table

Add a new `auth_flow` table to the auth-service SQLite:

```sql
CREATE TABLE auth_flow (
  flow_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  request_uri TEXT NOT NULL,
  client_id TEXT,
  email TEXT,
  created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
  expires_at INTEGER NOT NULL
);
```

Cleanup: delete expired rows on the same 5-minute interval as existing token cleanup.

### Bridge route: auth-service `GET /auth/complete`

This is the route better-auth redirects to after successful authentication. It is the `callbackURL` passed to better-auth's sign-in methods.

Steps:
1. Read `magic_auth_flow` cookie → get flow_id
2. Look up auth_flow row → get request_uri, client_id
3. Get better-auth session via `auth.api.getSession({ headers })`
4. Extract verified email from session
5. Check consent needed (reuse existing client_logins logic)
6. If consent needed → redirect to auth-service `/auth/consent?flow_id=...`
7. Otherwise → build HMAC-signed redirect URL:
   - Compute HMAC-SHA256(request_uri || email || approved || new_account || ts, MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET)
   - Redirect to pds-core `/oauth/magic-callback?...&ts=...&sig=...`
8. Delete auth_flow row + clear cookie

## Files to create

- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts` — the bridge route

## Files to modify

- `packages/shared/src/db.ts` — add auth_flow table creation/migration + CRUD methods
- `packages/auth-service/src/index.ts` — mount the /auth/complete route

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] auth_flow table created in auth-service SQLite
- [ ] Expired auth_flow rows cleaned up periodically
- [ ] Bridge route reads better-auth session and extracts verified email
- [ ] Bridge route produces HMAC-signed redirect to pds-core /oauth/magic-callback
- [ ] Consent flow still works (redirect to /auth/consent when needed)
- [ ] Cookie set/cleared correctly
close_reason: Added auth_flow table (v6 migration) to shared/db.ts with createAuthFlow/getAuthFlow/deleteAuthFlow/cleanupExpiredAuthFlows CRUD methods. Created packages/auth-service/src/routes/complete.ts bridge route that reads magic_auth_flow cookie, validates auth_flow record, gets better-auth session via fromNodeHeaders, determines isNewAccount via PDS internal endpoint, handles consent redirect, and issues HMAC-signed redirect to pds-core /oauth/magic-callback. Mounted in index.ts and wired cleanup in context.ts. 67 tests pass.

title: OTP email branding: support per-client templates
description:
The OTP email is currently hardcoded with Ma Earth branding. This needs to be fixed in one of two ways:

## Option A — Certified branding (simple)
Replace the Ma Earth branding with Certified branding as the default. Low effort, works for all clients but is not customisable.

## Option B — Per-client email templates (extensible)
Allow each trusted client app to provide its own email templates, which the auth service retrieves over HTTP at send time (or caches). The client would expose a well-known URL (e.g. `/.well-known/email-templates/otp.html`) that the auth service fetches and fills in with template variables (email address, OTP code, expiry, etc.).

This is analogous to how the HyperCerts PDS fork handles custom branding for trusted clients — the auth service fetches branding assets from the client's metadata URL.

## Considerations
- Option B requires a mechanism for the auth service to know which client triggered the OTP send (the `auth_flow` row already records the `client_id`/`request_uri`, so the client can be identified)
- Template fetching should be cached and have a timeout to avoid blocking OTP delivery
- Fallback to Certified default branding if the client provides no template
- Security: templates must be fetched only from trusted clients (already registered); content should be sanitised or restricted to a safe subset of HTML
- Option A can be done immediately as a stop-gap; Option B is the longer-term solution

## Reference
See how custom branding is handled in the HyperCerts PDS fork for the trusted client pattern.
close_reason: Already fully implemented. sender.ts fetches email_template_uri from client metadata (resolveClientMetadata), renders it with Mustache-style conditionals (renderTemplate), and falls back to default Certified templates. better-auth.ts resolves clientId from the auth_flow cookie and passes it to sendOtpCode. maearth-demo already serves email_template_uri pointing to /public/email-template.html with Ma Earth branding and {{code}}/{{app_name}}/{{is_new_user}} placeholders. The Ma Earth branded OTP email is the feature working correctly.

title: Flow 2 (app has login button, no login_hint) is untested and likely broken
description:
Flow 2 — where the client app has a simple 'Login' button and no email form of its own — has never been tested end-to-end. Only Flow 1 (login_hint passed via PAR) has been verified working.

## What Flow 2 is supposed to do

1. Client redirects to /oauth/authorize with no login_hint
2. Auth server renders the email input form (step-email div, visible by default)
3. User enters email, submits form
4. JS calls sendOtp(email) → POST /api/auth/email-otp/send-verification-otp
5. On success, JS calls showOtpStep(email) to transition to OTP input
6. User enters OTP code
7. JS calls verifyOtp(email, otp) → POST /api/auth/sign-in/email-otp
8. On success, redirects to /auth/complete
9. /auth/complete reads magic_auth_flow cookie → issues AT Protocol auth code via /oauth/magic-callback
10. Client exchanges code for tokens

## Current code state

The email form and JS transition path exists in login-page.ts (step-email div rendered
visible when no loginHint, form-send-otp submit handler calls sendOtp → showOtpStep).
However it has not been exercised and may have issues at any step.

## What needs investigating / testing

- Does a client without login_hint successfully redirect to /oauth/authorize?
- Does the email form render correctly?
- Does sendOtp succeed (better-auth endpoint reachable, correct request format)?
- Does showOtpStep transition work (CSS classes, currentEmail set correctly)?
- Does verifyOtp succeed and redirect to /auth/complete?
- Does /auth/complete correctly pick up the magic_auth_flow cookie in this path?
- Does magic-callback create the PDS account correctly for a new user?
- Does the whole token exchange complete?

## Reference implementation

The maearth-demo app at /data/projects/maearth-demo only implements Flow 1.
To test Flow 2, either modify maearth-demo to skip the login_hint, or use a
separate minimal client (e.g. curl + browser) that redirects to /oauth/authorize
without login_hint.

title: Replace /_magic/check-email with protected /_internal/account-by-email
description:
## Problem

The `/_magic/check-email` endpoint on pds-core returns `{ exists: true/false, did: "..." }` for any email with no authentication. It is an email enumeration oracle. It also reads from the `account_email` mirror table in `magic-pds.sqlite`, which we are eliminating as part of the broader migration to use `account.sqlite` as the single source of truth.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as MED-4.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.4

## Solution

Replace `/_magic/check-email` with `/_internal/account-by-email`, which:
- Requires `x-internal-secret` header matching `MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET` env var
- Queries `pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail()` directly — the upstream PDS already has this as a public method on AccountManager (in `node_modules/@atproto/pds/src/account-manager/account-manager.ts`). It queries `account.sqlite` directly — no mirror table needed.
- Returns `{ did: string } | { did: null }`

## Implementation

1. In `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts`, add the new endpoint:

```typescript
app.get("/_internal/account-by-email", async (req, res) => {
  if (req.headers["x-internal-secret"] !== process.env.MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET) {
    return res.status(401).json({ error: "Unauthorized" });
  }
  const email = req.query.email as string;
  if (!email) return res.status(400).json({ error: "Missing email" });
  const account = await pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail(email);
  res.json({ did: account?.did ?? null });
});
```

2. Update auth-service to call `/_internal/account-by-email` with `x-internal-secret` header instead of calling `/_magic/check-email`.

3. Delete `/_magic/check-email` from pds-core.

4. Add `MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET` to `.env.example`.

## Files to modify

- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts` — add new endpoint, delete old one
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/` (any file calling check-email) — update to new endpoint
- `.env.example` — add MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/_internal/account-by-email` requires x-internal-secret header
- [ ] Unauthenticated calls return 401
- [ ] Valid calls return `{ did: "..." }` or `{ did: null }`
- [ ] `/_magic/check-email` is deleted
- [ ] Auth-service uses the new endpoint successfully
close_reason: Added /_internal/account-by-email endpoint to pds-core requiring x-internal-secret header, querying pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccountByEmail() directly. Deleted /_magic/check-email. Updated auth-service account-login.ts to call the new protected endpoint. Added MAGIC_INTERNAL_SECRET to .env.example.

title: Stop assigning random passwords to new accounts
description:
## Problem

Accounts are created with a random 64-byte hex password (`crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('hex')`) in two places:
- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts:130`
- `packages/auth-service/src/lib/auto-provision.ts:20`

This password is never shown to users but is a valid credential for `com.atproto.server.createSession`. If it were ever leaked (logs, memory dump, DB access), it bypasses the entire auth service.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as MED-3.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.3

## Solution

Pass `password: undefined` when creating accounts. The PDS `accountManager.createAccount()` already handles this — it sets `passwordScrypt` to `undefined`, meaning:
- `createSession` rejects login (no hash to compare against)
- Password reset has nothing to reset
- Users who later want password-based login can use the stock PDS `requestPasswordReset` → `resetPassword` flow to set one — this works even when no password was previously set

## Implementation

1. In `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts:130`, change `password: randomBytes(64).toString('hex')` to `password: undefined`
2. In `packages/auth-service/src/lib/auto-provision.ts:20`, same change
3. Verify the PDS handles passwordless accounts correctly — test that `createSession` returns a proper error (not a crash) when there is no password hash

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] New accounts are created with no password
- [ ] `createSession` returns a clear error for passwordless accounts (not a 500)
- [ ] `requestPasswordReset` + `resetPassword` can set a password on a previously passwordless account
- [ ] Existing login flow via OTP is unaffected
close_reason: Removed random password generation from both auto-provision.ts (removed password field from XRPC call entirely since lexicon accepts optional) and pds-core/src/index.ts (used undefined as unknown as string to bypass SignUpInput schema type since no runtime schema validation at direct call site). Also removed unused crypto import from both files.

title: CRITICAL: Sign magic callback with HMAC-SHA256
description:
## Problem

The `/oauth/magic-callback` endpoint on pds-core accepts security-critical authorization decisions as plain, unsigned query parameters:

```
GET /oauth/magic-callback?request_uri=urn:...&email=victim@example.com&approved=1&new_account=0
```

An attacker who initiates a legitimate OAuth flow (getting a valid `request_uri` via PAR) can directly call the magic-callback with the victim's email instead of completing OTP verification. If the PDS port is reachable (misconfigured firewall, Docker port mapping, open redirect), any account can be taken over without any authentication factor.

**This is the most severe finding from the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities).**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.1

## Solution

Add a shared HMAC-SHA256 secret between auth-service and pds-core. The auth-service computes a signature over the callback parameters and a timestamp, appending `&ts=...&sig=...` to the redirect URL. pds-core verifies the signature before issuing an authorization code.

## Implementation

1. Add `MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET` env var (shared by both services). Generate with `openssl rand -hex 32`.

2. In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, add `signCallback()`:
   - Input: `{ request_uri, email, approved, new_account }` + secret
   - Compute `ts = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000)`
   - Payload: `[request_uri, email, approved, new_account, ts].join("\n")`
   - Return HMAC-SHA256 hex digest + timestamp

3. In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, add `verifyCallback()`:
   - Input: same params + ts + sig + secret
   - Reject if timestamp older than 5 minutes or in the future
   - Recompute HMAC and compare with `crypto.timingSafeEqual()`
   - Return boolean

4. In auth-service `routes/verify-code.ts` and `routes/consent.ts`: call `signCallback()` when building the redirect URL to `/oauth/magic-callback`. Append `&ts=...&sig=...`.

5. In pds-core `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts`, in the `/oauth/magic-callback` handler: call `verifyCallback()` before any account operations. Return 403 on failure, 400 on expiry.

6. Update `.env.example` with `MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET`.

## Files to modify

- `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts` — add signCallback/verifyCallback
- `packages/pds-core/src/index.ts` — add HMAC verification at top of magic-callback handler
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/verify-code.ts` — sign redirect URL
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/consent.ts` — sign redirect URL
- `.env.example` — add MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Unsigned requests to `/oauth/magic-callback` return 403
- [ ] Requests with expired timestamps (>5 min) return 400
- [ ] Requests with valid signatures proceed normally
- [ ] Comparison uses timingSafeEqual (no timing side-channel)
- [ ] Existing OTP login flow works end-to-end with HMAC in place
close_reason: Implemented HMAC-SHA256 signing for magic-callback: added signCallback/verifyCallback to shared/crypto.ts, updated verify-code.ts and consent.ts to sign redirects, added verification in pds-core magic-callback handler (returns 403/400 on invalid/expired sig), added MAGIC_CALLBACK_SECRET to .env.example, and added 9 new tests covering all verification paths.

title: Prevent flash of email login form when login_hint is present
description:
When /oauth/authorize is called with a login_hint email, the current implementation renders the full login page (including the email input step) and then client-side JS transitions to the OTP step after sending the OTP. This causes a brief flash of the email input form before the OTP step appears, which is confusing to users who already entered their email in the client app.

Fix: when login_hint is a valid email, send the OTP server-side during the GET /oauth/authorize handler and render the page already in the OTP step (hiding the email step entirely via HTML/CSS). No client-side transition needed — the OTP step should be immediately visible with no flash.
notes:
When this issue is resolved, also remove the 'known issue: brief flash of email form' caveat from README.md (Flow 1 description) and from docs/flows.md.

A partial fix exists in git stash: 'atproto-ke8: partial flash fix (server-side step visibility, OTP still client-side)'. What it achieves: renders step-email hidden and step-otp visible server-side when login_hint is present, pre-fills the masked email subtitle, pre-fills the hidden email input, and shows the recovery link. The flash is eliminated visually. What it does NOT do: OTP is still sent client-side via JS on page load (sendOtp called in DOMContentLoaded). The full fix requires moving OTP dispatch into the server-side GET handler before rendering the page. See atproto-trf for investigation into that approach.
close_reason: Implemented Method-Aware Hybrid approach: server-side initialStep rendering (no flash), client-side POST auto-send with accurate subtitle UX, otpAlreadySent flag for duplicate GET protection. Design doc and README/flows.md caveats updated.

title: Tighten domain boundary matching in /tls-check to prevent sibling domain spoofing
description:
## Files
- packages/pds-core/src/index.ts (modify)

## What to do
In the `checkHandleRoute()` function (~line 478), the current domain matching uses `domain.endsWith(avail)` which allows sibling domains (e.g. `evil-example.com` matches `example.com`). Fix this by:

1. Normalize the incoming `domain` query param before any checks:
   ```ts
   const normalizedDomain = domain.trim().toLowerCase().replace(/\.$/, '')
   ```
   Place this right after the `typeof domain !== 'string'` guard.

2. Replace the `find` + `endsWith` check with `some` + proper boundary matching:
   ```ts
   const isHostedHandle = pds.ctx.cfg.identity.serviceHandleDomains.some(
     (avail: string) => {
       const normalizedAvail = avail.toLowerCase()
       return (
         normalizedDomain === normalizedAvail ||
         normalizedDomain.endsWith(`.${normalizedAvail}`)
       )
     },
   )
   ```

3. Use `normalizedDomain` (instead of raw `domain`) in the `getAccount()` call below:
   ```ts
   const account = await pds.ctx.accountManager.getAccount(normalizedDomain)
   ```

4. Also use `normalizedDomain` in the hostname/auth checks above:
   ```ts
   if (normalizedDomain === pds.ctx.cfg.service.hostname || normalizedDomain === authHostname) {
   ```

## Don't
- Change any other function or route
- Add new dependencies
- Modify the Caddyfile
- Change the response shapes or status codes
acceptance_criteria:
1. `evil-example.com` does NOT match serviceHandleDomain `example.com`
2. `alice.example.com` DOES match serviceHandleDomain `example.com`
3. `example.com` exact match DOES work
4. Domain with trailing dot `alice.example.com.` DOES match
5. Domain with mixed case `Alice.Example.COM` DOES match
6. pnpm typecheck passes
7. pnpm format:check passes
8. pnpm lint passes

title: CRITICAL: Increase OTP to 8 digits and add per-email lockout
description:
## Problem

The current OTP system uses 6 digits (1,000,000 possibilities) with 5 attempts per token and 5 tokens per email per hour. This allows 25 guesses/hour. Over days or weeks this becomes non-trivial for a targeted attack against a specific account.

**Identified in the [magic-pds security review](https://gist.github.com/s-adamantine/c88df4fc940259cfe903e9de474f07fc#critical-vulnerabilities) as CRITICAL-2.**

See also: `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 0.2

## Solution

1. Increase OTP from 6 to 8 digits (100,000,000 possibilities — 100x harder brute-force)
2. Add a per-email lockout after N total failed attempts across all tokens (e.g., 15 total failures in 1 hour → lock the email for 1 hour)

## Implementation

### 8-digit OTP (current codebase)

In `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts`, change `generateOtpCode()` to generate 8-digit codes instead of 6-digit codes. This is a single-line change to the `randomInt()` range.

Update all email templates in `packages/auth-service/src/email/sender.ts` to accommodate 8-digit codes (the letter-spacing and font-size in the HTML templates may need slight adjustment).

### 8-digit OTP (after better-auth migration)

Set `otpLength: 8` in the `emailOTP` plugin configuration in `packages/auth-service/src/better-auth.ts`.

### Per-email lockout

Add a new column or table tracking total failed attempts per email across all OTP tokens within a time window. When 15 failures occur within 1 hour, reject new OTP verification attempts for that email for 1 hour. This is orthogonal to the existing per-token attempt limit (5 per token).

## Files to modify

- `packages/shared/src/crypto.ts` — change OTP digit count
- `packages/auth-service/src/email/sender.ts` — update email templates for 8-digit display
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/rate-limit.ts` or `packages/shared/src/db.ts` — add per-email lockout logic

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] OTP codes are 8 digits
- [ ] Email templates display 8 digits correctly
- [ ] Per-email lockout activates after 15 total failed attempts in 1 hour
- [ ] Lockout prevents new OTP verification (not sending — user can still request codes)
- [ ] Existing login flow works with 8-digit codes
close_reason: Increased OTP from 6 to 8 digits (100M possibilities), added per-email lockout (15 failures/hour → 1-hour lock) via new otp_failed_attempts table in db.ts with migration v4, updated token.ts to check and record failures, updated email templates for 8-digit display, added lockout test.

title: Build unified login page with multiple auth methods
description:
## Context

Phase 2 of the better-auth migration plan. See `better-auth-migration-plan.md` Phase 2.4.

## Problem

The current auth-service login flow is hardcoded for email OTP only (authorize.ts → send-code.ts → verify-code.ts). It needs to become a landing page offering multiple login methods.

## Solution

Replace the existing `GET /oauth/authorize` on the auth-service with a unified login page that:

1. Receives `?request_uri=...&client_id=...&prompt=...&login_hint=...` from the pds-core AS metadata redirect
2. Creates an `auth_flow` row (flow_id, request_uri, client_id)
3. Sets `magic_auth_flow` cookie (10 min, httpOnly)
4. Resolves client metadata for branding via existing `lib/client-metadata.ts` (logo, colors, name)
5. Renders a login page with:
   - Email OTP form (calls better-auth `/api/auth/*` endpoints on auth-service)
   - Social login buttons (only for providers enabled via env vars — check exported `socialProviders` object from `better-auth.ts`)
   - "Recover with backup email" link (to existing recovery flow)

### Social provider buttons

The login page template conditionally renders buttons based on `socialProviders`:
```typescript
if ("google" in socialProviders) { /* render Google button */ }
if ("github" in socialProviders) { /* render GitHub button */ }
```

Social login redirects to better-auth `/api/auth/sign-in/{provider}`, which handles the full OAuth exchange and redirects back to `/auth/complete` (the bridge route).

### Delete on completion

After this is working, delete the old files:
- `routes/authorize.ts`, `routes/send-code.ts`, `routes/verify-code.ts`
- `magic-link/token.ts`, `magic-link/rate-limit.ts`
- `middleware/rate-limit.ts`, `middleware/csrf.ts`
- Drop `magic_link_token` table from auth-service SQLite

Also remove `tokenService` and `rateLimiter` from `context.ts`.

## Files to create

- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts` — unified login page

## Files to modify

- `packages/auth-service/src/index.ts` — mount new /oauth/authorize, remove old routes
- `packages/auth-service/src/context.ts` — remove tokenService, rateLimiter

## Files to delete

- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/send-code.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/routes/verify-code.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/token.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/magic-link/rate-limit.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/middleware/rate-limit.ts`
- `packages/auth-service/src/middleware/csrf.ts`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Login page renders with email OTP form
- [ ] Social login buttons appear only for configured providers
- [ ] Client branding (logo, name, colors) displayed from OAuth metadata
- [ ] Email OTP flow works end-to-end through better-auth
- [ ] Social login (if configured) works end-to-end through better-auth → /auth/complete bridge
- [ ] Old OTP route files deleted
- [ ] Old middleware files deleted
- [ ] context.ts cleaned up

Client branding applied. Deleted old authorize.ts, send-code.ts, verify-code.ts. Removed tokenService/rateLimiter from context.ts, deleted magic-link/ directory. 10 login-page tests added, 83 tests total pass.

title: Eliminate auth server redirect in flow 1 (app has own email form)
description:
In flow 1, where the client app collects the user's email itself and passes it as login_hint, there is no reason to redirect through /oauth/authorize at all before showing the OTP input. The current flow is:

1. App collects email
2. App POSTs to /api/oauth/login → PAR → redirects to /oauth/authorize?login_hint=email
3. Auth server renders login page, JS auto-sends OTP, transitions to OTP step
4. User enters OTP

Step 3 is unnecessary indirection. Ideally the app could redirect directly to an OTP input page on the auth server, skipping the intermediate login page entirely. This would require a dedicated endpoint (e.g. /oauth/authorize/otp-challenge) that accepts login_hint, sends OTP server-side, and renders the OTP input directly — no login form involved at all.
notes: Even if the redirect could be eliminated for OTP-based login, it would need to be retained for other authentication mechanisms (e.g. passkeys). The AT Protocol OAuth spec requires the PAR + redirect to /oauth/authorize flow. So any work here should not remove the redirect as a code path — at most it could add a shortcut for OTP specifically while keeping the general redirect path available. Consider whether the complexity is worth it given passkeys will need the redirect anyway.
close_reason: Won't fix. The redirect through /oauth/authorize is mandated by the AT Protocol OAuth spec (PAR → request_uri → /oauth/authorize), not optional indirection — it cannot be skipped without breaking the protocol. Additionally, the redirect is the correct place for credential selection: assuming email OTP before consulting the user's registered credentials would need to be unwound when passkeys or social-only accounts are added. Both points are documented in docs/design/otp-send-client-vs-server.md.

title: Investigate server-side OTP send on login_hint (magic-pds vs current approach)
description:
The old magic-pds branch handled login_hint by sending the OTP server-side in the GET /oauth/authorize handler and rendering the OTP form directly as a full-page response (no flash, no JS transition needed). See authorize.ts on the magic-pds branch.

The current main branch unified everything into a single page (login-page.ts) with CSS show/hide and a JS-driven OTP send on page load. The uncommitted changes in login-page.ts are a partial improvement (pre-render OTP step server-side) but still send the OTP client-side via JS, so the subtitle 'We sent a code to...' is inaccurate at render time.

The proper fix is likely to restore the server-side OTP send approach from magic-pds, but the unified single-page architecture is different enough that this needs careful thought.

Investigation needed (requires owner input):

1. Produce Mermaid sequence diagrams of both approaches (blocked on atproto-n83 docs restructure, then atproto-y1l)
2. Review the diagrams with the owner to decide which approach to adopt
3. Decide what to do with the current uncommitted changes in login-page.ts (revert, keep, or replace)
4. Implement the agreed approach

Key constraint: the unified single-page architecture may make a pure server-side send harder (better-auth OTP send is async and involves HTTP calls to the better-auth backend), so tradeoffs need discussion.
close_reason: Investigation complete. The Method-Aware Hybrid approach was chosen (design doc updated by PR #1) and implemented in atproto-ke8. Server-side GET sends no OTP; client-side POST fires after pre-rendered OTP form. Subtitle inaccuracy fixed (shows 'Sending...' until POST succeeds).

title: Double OTP send: /oauth/authorize creates new auth_flow on every GET, needs idempotency for reused request_uri
description:
During testing, two OTP emails were sent for a single login attempt. The initial hypothesis was that the StayFocusd browser extension was making a duplicate GET request to /oauth/authorize, triggering a second auth_flow and a second OTP send.

However, the root cause may be different: the login flow is initiated by the client app making a GET request to /oauth/authorize, and it is possible that:
1. The request from the client app to initiate the login flow should be a POST, not a GET
2. ePDS should reject GET requests to /oauth/authorize and only accept POST

If (1) is true, StayFocusd following the GET redirect URL would be harmless (since ePDS would reject it). If (2) is already enforced and was not the issue, the root cause is elsewhere.

The duplicate request that was observed came from a different IP and user-agent than the real browser. This is consistent with StayFocusd, but could also be another extension, a proxy, or a prefetch mechanism.

## Investigation needed

The Mermaid sequence diagrams (atproto-y1l) will clarify exactly what HTTP method is used at each step of both flows and should be reviewed before drawing conclusions.

Questions to answer:
- What HTTP method does the AT Protocol spec require for the authorization endpoint redirect?
- Does ePDS currently accept GET, POST, or both for /oauth/authorize?
- Is the duplicate request coming from before or after the PAR redirect?
- If the fix is to require POST, what changes are needed in the client app (maearth-demo)?

## Observed symptom
- Two OTP emails sent to the same address for a single login attempt
- Duplicate request from different IP/user-agent than the real browser
- Only observed with StayFocusd extension installed; not reproduced in incognito
notes:
AT Protocol spec investigation complete: GET is the correct HTTP method for /oauth/authorize. The browser redirect to the authorization endpoint is inherently a GET per the OAuth 2.0 / PAR spec (RFC 9126). Requiring POST would be spec-violating.

The real issue: /oauth/authorize creates a new auth_flow row on every GET request, regardless of whether the request_uri has already been processed. If StayFocusd (or any other agent) makes a second GET to the same /oauth/authorize?request_uri=... URL, a second auth_flow is created and a second OTP is sent.

Correct fix: /oauth/authorize should be idempotent for a given request_uri. If a request_uri has already been consumed (or has an existing auth_flow), the second GET should either:
(a) Return the existing auth_flow / OTP step without sending a new OTP, or
(b) Return a 400 error rejecting the reused request_uri.

This is an atproto/oauth-provider behaviour question — need to check what the library does and whether there is a hook to intercept duplicate requests.
close_reason: Fixed by deduplicating GET /oauth/authorize on request_uri. getAuthFlowByRequestUri() added to MagicPdsDb; handler reuses existing flow and logs a warn with IP/UA when a duplicate is detected. New PAR calls always produce a fresh request_uri so unrelated logins and the Resend button are unaffected.

title: Fix: logo_uri from client metadata may be blocked by CSP img-src (from ePDS-aq2.4)
description: Review of ePDS-aq2.4 found: The login page now renders `` when the OAuth client provides a logo_uri in their metadata. However, the CSP img-src header (index.ts line 62-65) only allows images from 'self', 'data:', and the client_id origin. The logo_uri may be hosted on a different domain than the client_id. For example: - client_id: https://app.example.com/client-metadata.json - logo_uri: https://cdn.example.com/logo.png In this case, the CSP img-src would allow app.example.com but not cdn.example.com, causing the logo to be blocked with a CSP violation. Evidence: - index.ts line 48-65: CSP img-src is built from client_id origin only - login-page.ts line 211-213: logo_uri is rendered directly from client metadata - client-metadata.ts line 10: logo_uri is an unvalidated string from the fetched JSON Fix: When building the CSP header, also extract the origin from logo_uri (if present in the resolved client metadata) and add it to img-src. The logo_uri should be validated as an https:// URL before being added to CSP. Alternatively, proxy the logo through the auth service to avoid the cross-origin issue.

title: Fix: recovery .btn-primary has width:auto but login-page .btn-primary has no width — CSS divergence (from ePDS-aq2.5)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.5 found: The fac3f8d commit changed .btn-primary in recovery.ts to width:auto (line 675), but login-page.ts has no width property on .btn-primary at all (line 583-603). This is a CSS divergence between the two files.

Evidence:
- recovery.ts line 675: width: auto; (inside .btn-primary rule)
- login-page.ts: no width property on .btn-primary

In login-page.ts, the button is inside .otp-actions (flex column, align-items: flex-end) which naturally sizes it to content. In recovery.ts, the button is inside .form-actions (flex row, justify-content: space-between) where width:auto is correct to prevent it from stretching.

The divergence is intentional for the form-actions layout, but it means the CSS is no longer 'identical to login-page.ts' as the spec requires. This should be documented or the login-page.ts should also adopt width:auto for consistency.

Fix: Either document why width:auto is needed in recovery but not login-page, or add width:auto to login-page.ts .btn-primary as well (since inline-flex with auto width is the correct default for a button that should size to its content).

title: Fix: CSP img-src only allows AUTH_LOGO_URL origin, not client logo_uri origin (from ePDS-wae.10)
description: In index.ts (line 51-56), the CSP img-src is extended with the AUTH_LOGO_URL origin. However, the login page also renders client logo images from b.logo_uri (OAuth client metadata, line 224). If a client's logo_uri is on a different origin than AUTH_LOGO_URL, the image will be blocked by CSP. The client_id origin is added to img-src (lines 58-67) but the logo_uri may be on a CDN or different domain than the client_id URL. Fix: also fetch the logo_uri from client metadata in the CSP middleware and add its origin to img-src, or add it dynamically per-request when resolving client metadata.

title: Fix: recovery email input missing autocomplete="email" attribute (from ePDS-aq2.5)

title: Security: CSS injection via AUTH_BRAND_COLOR / AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR / AUTH_PANEL_COLOR env vars (from ePDS-wae.10)
description:
## Problem

Review of ePDS-wae.10 (commit 9fee49a) found a CSS injection vulnerability.

The three color env vars are inserted directly into CSS custom property values inside a `style` attribute and a `<style>` block:

```ts
// index.ts line 242
rootStyleProps += `--color-primary:${escapeHtml(brandColor)};--color-primary-contrast:#fff;`
// index.ts line 245
rootStyleProps += `--color-panel:${escapeHtml(panelColor)};--color-panel-text:#fff;...`
// login-page.ts line 253
const bgColorStyle = bgColor
  ? `<style>body { background: ${escapeHtml(bgColor)} !important; }</style>`
  : ''
```

`escapeHtml()` only escapes HTML entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `"`). It does NOT escape CSS-special characters: `;`, `}`, `(`, `)`, `url(`.

A malicious operator (or misconfigured deployment) setting:
```
AUTH_BRAND_COLOR=#3E7053; background: url(https://evil.com/track.gif)
AUTH_BACKGROUND_COLOR=red} body::after{content:url(https://evil.com/x)
AUTH_PANEL_COLOR=#3E7053; --color-panel-text: red
```

...would inject arbitrary CSS into the login page. For the `<style>` block (bgColorStyle), this is a full CSS injection — an attacker can exfiltrate form data via CSS attribute selectors + background-image requests.

## Evidence

- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts lines 241-253
- packages/auth-service/src/index.ts lines 141-143
- packages/shared/src/html.ts — escapeHtml() only handles HTML entities, not CSS

## Fix

Add a CSS color validator before using these values. Accept only:
1. Hex colors: `/^#[0-9a-fA-F]{3,8}$/`
2. Named CSS colors from a whitelist
3. `rgb()` / `hsl()` with numeric-only arguments validated by regex

Reject (log warning, fall back to default) any value that doesn't match. Apply this in `main()` in index.ts when reading env vars, before storing in config.
close_reason: 814f241 security: validate branding env vars to prevent CSS injection

title: Fix: verifyOtp uses currentEmail which is empty string when OTP step is server-rendered (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: When initialStep='otp' (login_hint provided), the JS sets currentEmail = loginHint at line 1054. However, the form-verify-otp submit handler at line 1007 calls verifyOtp(currentEmail, otp). If the user somehow submits the form before the JS initialization block runs (e.g. very fast submit, or if the initialization is at the bottom of the script), currentEmail would be '' (its initial value at line 891). In practice the script runs synchronously so this is unlikely, but it is fragile. More critically: if the user navigates back to the OTP step via btn-back and then forward again, currentEmail is correctly set by showOtpStep(). But on the initial server-rendered OTP step, currentEmail is set from loginHint (line 1054) which is correct. The hidden input #otp-email also has the email value. The verifyOtp function should read from otpEmailInput.value as a fallback: var email = currentEmail || otpEmailInput.value;

title: Security: AUTH_LOGO_URL not validated — allows tracking pixel from arbitrary domain (from ePDS-wae.10)
description: ## Problem Review of ePDS-wae.10 (commit 9fee49a) found that AUTH_LOGO_URL is accepted without any URL validation. In packages/auth-service/src/index.ts: ```ts logoUrl: process.env.AUTH_LOGO_URL || undefined, ``` In packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts: ```ts const logoHtml = logoUrl ? `` : '' ``` And in packages/auth-service/src/index.ts CSP middleware: ```ts if (ctx.config.logoUrl) { const logoOrigin = new URL(ctx.config.logoUrl).origin if (logoOrigin && logoOrigin !== 'null') imgSrc += ` ${logoOrigin}` } ``` Issues: 1. No scheme validation — a `javascript:` URL would be placed in `src` (browsers block it for `` but it's still bad practice) 2. No hostname validation — an operator could accidentally set this to a tracking pixel URL (e.g. from a third-party analytics service), which would then be whitelisted in CSP and loaded on every login page view, leaking user visit data to that third party 3. The URL is accepted as-is from the environment without any sanitization beyond HTML-escaping ## Evidence - packages/auth-service/src/index.ts lines 51-56, 140 - packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts lines 222-225 ## Fix In `main()` in index.ts, validate AUTH_LOGO_URL before storing: 1. Must be a valid URL (parseable by `new URL()`) 2. Must use `https:` scheme only 3. Log a warning and set to `undefined` if invalid This is operator-controlled so severity is lower than the CSS injection issue, but it's a defense-in-depth gap.

title: Fix: missing aria-live region for dynamic error/success messages (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: The #error-msg div has role='alert' (line 802) which is correct for errors. However, when showSuccess() is called (e.g. 'Code resent!'), the element still has role='alert' which is appropriate for assertive announcements. But the element starts with display:none and is shown dynamically — screen readers may not announce content injected into a hidden element that is then shown. The pattern should use aria-live='polite' for success messages and aria-live='assertive' (or role='alert') for errors. Since the same element is reused for both, consider splitting into two separate elements, or dynamically toggling aria-live between 'polite' and 'assertive' in showError()/showSuccess().

title: Fix: resend button race condition allows double OTP send on initial auto-send (from ePDS-aq2.1)
Security: user-controlled client_id weakens CSP img-src on every page load (from ePDS-wae.10)
description:
## Problem

Review of ePDS-wae.10 (commit 9fee49a) found a CSP weakening issue in the security headers middleware added in packages/auth-service/src/index.ts (lines 57-67).

The code adds the origin of the `client_id` query/body parameter to the `img-src` CSP directive on every request:

```ts
const clientId = (req.query.client_id as string) || req.body?.client_id
if (clientId && typeof clientId === 'string') {
  try {
    const clientOrigin = new URL(clientId).origin
    if (clientOrigin && clientOrigin !== 'null') {
      imgSrc += ` ${clientOrigin}`
    }
  } catch { /* not a valid URL, keep default */ }
}
```

`client_id` is a user-supplied query parameter. Any visitor can pass `?client_id=https://evil.com` to add `https://evil.com` to the `img-src` directive for their own page load. This:

1. Allows loading tracking pixels from arbitrary origins on the login page
2. Weakens the CSP for that specific request (though only for images)
3. Could be used to probe internal network origins if the auth service is accessible from a network where SSRF is possible

## Evidence

- packages/auth-service/src/index.ts lines 57-67
- The `client_id` parameter is not validated against any allowlist before being used to expand CSP

## Context

The intent is to allow the OAuth client's logo (from `logo_uri` in client metadata) to load. However, the client metadata fetch already happens in `resolveClientMetadata()` in login-page.ts — the actual logo origin could be extracted there and passed to the CSP builder, rather than trusting the raw `client_id` URL.

## Fix

Option A (preferred): Remove the `client_id`-based CSP expansion entirely. Instead, after resolving client metadata in login-page.ts, extract the `logo_uri` origin and set a response header (e.g. `X-Logo-Origin`) that the CSP middleware can read. Or pass the resolved logo origin directly to a per-response CSP setter.

Option B (minimal): Validate that `client_id` is a registered/known client before adding its origin to CSP. Since ATProto client_ids are URLs to metadata documents, this means fetching the metadata first (which login-page.ts already does) and only then allowing the logo_uri origin.

title: Fix: OTP step subtitle empty on initial server-render when login_hint present but otpAlreadySent=false (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: When initialStep='otp' and otpAlreadySent=false (first load with login_hint), the server renders the otp-subtitle paragraph as empty (line 846-850 renders empty string). The JS then fires sendOtp() and on success sets otpSubtitle.textContent = 'We sent an 8-digit code to ' + masked. However, there is a race: if the page renders and the user immediately looks at the OTP field, the subtitle is blank until the fetch completes. More importantly, if JS is disabled or the fetch fails silently, the subtitle stays blank forever. Fix: render the subtitle server-side as 'Sending code to ${masked}...' and let JS update it to 'We sent...' on success.
close_reason: 171adf1 fix: show 'Sending code to...' subtitle during OTP auto-send

title: Epic: Resolve CodeRabbit review for PR #10
description: Resolve open CodeRabbit inline review comments on PR #10. 2 actionable comments across 2 files — both about replacing inline display style toggles with CSS classes per AGENTS.md rules.

title: Replace inline display toggles with CSS .hidden class
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (modify)
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/recovery.ts (modify)

## What to do
Replace all inline `style="display:..."` attributes and `element.style.display` JS mutations with CSS class-based visibility, per AGENTS.md rule: "Use CSS classes to control visibility (hidden, active) — avoid inline display style except for dynamic values set at render time."

### login-page.ts changes

**1. Add `.hidden` CSS rule** in the `<style>` block (near the existing `.step-otp` rule):
```css
.hidden { display: none !important; }
```

**2. Replace static inline display attributes in HTML:**

- `<div id="error-msg" class="admonition error" style="display:none;">` → `<div id="error-msg" class="admonition error hidden">`
- `<span class="admonition-icon admonition-icon-success" style="display:none;" ...>` → `<span class="admonition-icon admonition-icon-success hidden" ...>`
- `<div id="social-section"${opts.initialStep === 'otp' ? ' style="display:none;"' : ''}>` → `<div id="social-section"${opts.initialStep === 'otp' ? ' class="hidden"' : ''}>`

**3. Replace JS `style.display` mutations with classList:**

In the `showError()` function:
- `iconError.style.display = ''` → `iconError.classList.remove('hidden')`
- `iconSuccess.style.display = 'none'` → `iconSuccess.classList.add('hidden')`
- `errorEl.style.display = 'flex'` → `errorEl.classList.remove('hidden')`

In the `showSuccess()` function:
- `iconError.style.display = 'none'` → `iconError.classList.add('hidden')`
- `iconSuccess.style.display = ''` → `iconSuccess.classList.remove('hidden')`
- `errorEl.style.display = 'flex'` → `errorEl.classList.remove('hidden')`

In the `clearError()` function:
- `errorEl.style.display = 'none'` → `errorEl.classList.add('hidden')`

In the `showOtpStep()` function:
- `if (socialSection) socialSection.style.display = 'none'` → `if (socialSection) socialSection.classList.add('hidden')`

In the `showEmailStep()` function:
- `if (socialSection) socialSection.style.display = ''` → `if (socialSection) socialSection.classList.remove('hidden')`

**Important:** The `.hidden` class needs `!important` because the admonition normally has `display: flex` — without `!important`, the class-based hide would lose to the more specific `.admonition` rule. Alternatively, increase specificity: `.admonition.hidden { display: none; }` — either approach works.

### recovery.ts changes

**1. Add `.inline-form` CSS rule** in the `getBaseCSS()` function:
```css
.inline-form { display: inline; }
```

**2. Replace inline style on the resend form:**
- `<form method="POST" action="/auth/recover" style="display:inline;">` → `<form method="POST" action="/auth/recover" class="inline-form">`

This appears in the `renderOtpForm()` function.

## Don't
- Do NOT change any route handler logic
- Do NOT change any functionality — only the mechanism for showing/hiding elements
- Do NOT remove the `.step-otp` or `.step-email` class-based visibility (those already follow the pattern)
- Do NOT change the admonition's visible state styling (it should still render as flex when visible)
acceptance_criteria:
1. `pnpm typecheck` passes
2. `pnpm build` succeeds
3. Zero occurrences of `style="display:none;"` in login-page.ts (grep returns nothing)
4. Zero occurrences of `style="display:inline;"` in recovery.ts
5. Zero occurrences of `.style.display` in login-page.ts JS (grep for "style.display" returns nothing)
6. A `.hidden` CSS rule exists in login-page.ts
7. An `.inline-form` CSS rule exists in recovery.ts
8. The error-msg div uses class="admonition error hidden" (not style="display:none")
9. The admonition-icon-success span uses the hidden class (not style="display:none")
10. The social-section div uses class="hidden" when initialStep is otp (not style="display:none")
11. JS showError/showSuccess/clearError use classList.add/remove("hidden") not style.display
12. All existing show/hide behavior is preserved — elements still appear and disappear correctly
close_reason: f00a01e Replace inline display toggles with CSS .hidden class

title: Epic: Restyle login page to match Bluesky OAuth UI (vanilla, no custom branding)
description:

Restyle the auth-service login page (GET /oauth/authorize) to match the Bluesky `@atproto/oauth-provider-ui` design. This is a clean baseline — no Certified-specific branding, no GainForest logos, no custom SVGs. Just the standard atproto OAuth look.

## What success looks like

- CSS custom property token system matching atproto's (--hue-primary, --color-contrast-*, --color-primary-*)
- Dark mode support via prefers-color-scheme with inverted contrast scale
- Input components styled like atproto's InputContainer (rounded-lg, bg-gray-100, focus ring)

## Context
Current branch (bsky-signin-page) has the old basic centered-card design (513 lines). The ui-design branch has a Certified-branded version. This epic creates the generic atproto-style baseline that can later be themed per-tenant.

title: Fix recovery link to pass actual PAR request_uri instead of pdsPublicUrl
description:
## Files
- packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts (modify)

## What to do
The recovery link passes `opts.pdsPublicUrl` as the `request_uri` query parameter, but it should pass the actual PAR `request_uri` (e.g. `urn:ietf:params:oauth:request_uri:...`). This is needed so the recovery flow can resume the correct OAuth session.

### Changes needed:

1. **Add `requestUri` to the `renderLoginPage` opts type** (line ~196-206):
   Add `requestUri: string` to the opts interface.

2. **Pass `requestUri` from the route handler** (line ~188):
   The route handler already has `requestUri` in scope (from `req.query.request_uri`). Add it to the `renderLoginPage()` call:
   ```ts
   res.type("html").send(
     renderLoginPage({
       flowId,
       clientId: clientId ?? "",
       clientName,
       branding: clientMeta,
       loginHint: emailHint,
       initialStep,
       otpAlreadySent,
       csrfToken: res.locals.csrfToken,
       authBasePath: "/api/auth",
       pdsPublicUrl: ctx.config.pdsPublicUrl,
       requestUri,  // <-- ADD THIS
     }),
   );
   ```
   Note: `requestUri` is guaranteed to be a string at this point because the route handler returns early with an error if it is missing (line ~51).

3. **Update the recovery link** (line ~827):
   Change from:
   ```
   /auth/recover?request_uri=${encodeURIComponent(opts.pdsPublicUrl)}
   ```
   To:
   ```
   /auth/recover?request_uri=${encodeURIComponent(opts.requestUri)}
   ```

### Reference
See the ui-design branch for the correct implementation: `git show ui-design:packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts | grep -n "requestUri"`

## Don't
- Do NOT change any CSS or HTML layout
- Do NOT remove pdsPublicUrl from opts (it may be used elsewhere)
- Do NOT change any other route handlers or files
acceptance_criteria:

title: Fix dark mode placeholder to use CSS token instead of hardcoded gray

Keep that — it works correctly in both modes because `--color-text-light` maps to `--color-contrast-700` which inverts in dark mode.

- Do NOT change any other CSS rules
- Do NOT change any other files

title: Fix: brandColor comparison uses hardcoded default '#8338ec' instead of constant (from ePDS-wae.10)
description: In renderLoginPage() at line 241, the brand color override is only applied when brandColor !== '#8338ec'. This hardcoded string is the default value. If the default is ever changed (e.g. to match a different atproto version), this comparison will silently break — the override will always be applied even when using the default. Fix: extract the default as a named constant (e.g. const DEFAULT_BRAND_COLOR = '#8338ec') and use it in both the fallback assignment (line 219) and the comparison (line 241).

title: Fix title-panel inner content alignment: justify-items:end on .title-panel, justify-items:start on .title-panel-inner at 768px breakpoint
description: The previous worker changed justify-items on .title-panel from end to start, which moved the entire content block to the left side of the panel. The fix: revert .title-panel desktop to justify-items:end and text-align:right (positions block on right side of panel), and set .title-panel-inner to justify-items:start and text-align:left (left-aligns items within the block relative to each other). Only the @media (min-width: 768px) block in login-page.ts needs changing.

title: Fix: .btn-primary missing hover state (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: Review of the login page restyle found that .btn-primary has no :hover CSS rule. The button transitions with 'transition: all 0.3s ease-in-out' but there is nothing to transition to on hover — the button gives no visual feedback when hovered. The atproto reference UI uses a darkened/lightened primary color on hover. Fix: add .btn-primary:hover { filter: brightness(0.9); } or equivalent. This also affects keyboard focus — .btn-primary:focus-visible uses outline:2px solid var(--color-primary) which is invisible when the button background IS the primary color. The outline-offset:2px helps but the outline color should be a contrasting color (e.g. white or a darker shade).
close_reason: 1986f53 fix: add .btn-primary:hover:not(:disabled) opacity rule

title: Fix: btn-primary focus-visible outline invisible on primary-color background (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: The .btn-primary:focus-visible rule sets outline:2px solid var(--color-primary) with outline-offset:2px. When the button background is var(--color-primary) (e.g. #8338ec), the outline is the same color as the button — effectively invisible. WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.11 (Non-text Contrast) requires a 3:1 contrast ratio for focus indicators. Fix: use a contrasting color for the focus outline, e.g. outline:2px solid var(--color-primary-contrast) or a dark ring. A double-ring approach (box-shadow) like the input-container uses would be more visible: box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px var(--color-primary), 0 0 0 4px var(--color-contrast-0).
close_reason: 1986f53 fix: change focus-visible outline to var(--color-contrast-1000)

title: Fix: social login callbackURL hardcoded to /auth/complete, not flow-aware (from ePDS-wae.1)
description: The social login buttons link to /api/auth/sign-in/social?provider=google&callbackURL=/auth/complete (lines 268, 282). The callbackURL is hardcoded to /auth/complete. The /auth/complete route reads the epds_auth_flow cookie to get the flow_id and resume the AT Protocol flow. This works IF the cookie is set before the social login redirect. The cookie IS set in the GET /oauth/authorize handler (line 108). However, after the social OAuth round-trip (Google/GitHub → callback → better-auth → /auth/complete), the browser may have lost the epds_auth_flow cookie if the social provider's redirect clears cookies or if SameSite=Lax causes the cookie to not be sent on the cross-site redirect back. This is a known issue with SameSite=Lax and OAuth redirects. The flow_id should be passed as a query param in the callbackURL as a fallback: callbackURL=/auth/complete?flow_id=${flowId}.

title: Fix: dynamic role=alert assignment may not be announced by older screen readers (from ePDS-aq2.1)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.1 found: the error/success admonition div starts with no role attribute and style='display:none'. The showError() and showSuccess() JS functions call errorEl.setAttribute('role', 'alert'/'status') before setting display='flex'. 

The problem: NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver register ARIA live regions at page load time. Dynamically adding role='alert' to an element that was not a live region at load time is not reliably announced by all screen reader/browser combinations. The W3C ARIA authoring practices recommend having the live region present in the DOM from page load (even if empty/hidden) so screen readers register it.

Evidence: https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/alert/ — 'It is important to note that the alert role is not appropriate for dialog boxes. Instead, use the alertdialog role.'  Also: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Accessibility/ARIA/Roles/alert_role — 'Do not dynamically add role=alert to an element.'

Fix: Add role='alert' aria-live='assertive' aria-atomic='true' to the #error-msg div in the initial HTML (alongside style='display:none'). Remove the setAttribute/removeAttribute calls from showError/showSuccess/clearError. Screen readers will then announce content changes when the element becomes visible.

title: Fix: title panel h1 CSS selector mismatch — .title class has no CSS definition (from ePDS-aq2.4)
description:
Review of ePDS-aq2.4 found: The HTML renders `

` (line 711) but the CSS targets `.title-panel h1` (line 403) — not `.title`. The `.title` class has no CSS definition anywhere in the stylesheet. Evidence: - Line 711: `

Sign in with Certified

` - Line 403: `.title-panel h1 { font-size: 20px; ... }` — targets h1 by tag, not by class - No `.title { ... }` rule exists in the stylesheet The styling still works because `.title-panel h1` matches any h1 inside .title-panel. However: 1. The `.title` class is dead markup — it does nothing 2. The ePDS-aq2.4 spec says to add `.title { ... }` CSS rules (font-size: 20px, font-weight: 600, etc.) 3. The CSS selector approach is inconsistent — the spec intended a `.title` class with its own rules, not a descendant selector Fix: Either add a `.title { ... }` CSS rule matching the spec, or remove the `class="title"` from the h1 (since the descendant selector already works). The former is preferred for consistency with the spec.

Change to: primary button full-width on top, then the two secondary buttons side by side below. Files: packages/auth-service/src/routes/login-page.ts only.

title: Epic: PDS-level branding — atproto-style theming via env vars
description:
## Goal
Bring ePDS auth-service theming to parity with atproto's oauth-provider customization system. A PDS operator should be able to set PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR (and optionally PDS_SERVICE_NAME, PDS_LOGO_URL, PDS_SIGN_IN_TEXT, PDS_TERMS_OF_SERVICE_URL, PDS_PRIVACY_POLICY_URL) and have ALL auth-service pages consistently themed.

## Context
- Currently the login page hardcodes #1A130F as brand color and #F2EBE4 as background. Recovery uses #0f1828. Consent/account pages use yet another set of hardcoded colors. There are 6 templates with 3 different CSS systems.
- atproto's pattern: PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR → parse hex → auto-compute contrast (WCAG luminance) + hue (for HSL palette) → inject :root CSS vars → all pages themed.
- PR #9 (css-injection-trusted-clients) adds per-client CSS overrides for trusted clients. This epic adds the PDS-operator-level defaults that sit underneath client overrides in the cascade.
- The ui-design branch had AUTH_BRAND_COLOR etc. but was never merged and used a simpler approach. This epic replaces that with the atproto pattern using PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR.

## Key Constraints
- Use PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR (not AUTH_BRAND_COLOR) since pds-core already has this env var available from the atproto fork.
- Auto-compute contrast color (black or white) via WCAG relative luminance. Auto-extract hue for HSL palette generation.
- All 6 auth-service page templates must use a single shared CSS module.
- Client CSS from PR #9 must cascade AFTER PDS branding (client overrides operator).
- No breaking changes to existing env vars.

## Success
- Set PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR=#2cce8a → all auth pages (login, recovery, consent, account-login, account-settings, error pages) use green primary with auto-computed dark contrast text.
- Unset PDS_PRIMARY_COLOR → pages use sensible defaults (current #1A130F).
- Trusted client branding.css still overrides PDS defaults.
